name: SSH Connect
description: Open or close an SSH connection to a remote server. Once connected, bash and runpy tools execute on the remote server instead of locally.

function.name: sshc
function.description:
Manage an SSH connection for this session. Use action='open' to connect to a remote server — after that, all bash and runpy tool calls will execute on that server. Use action='close' to disconnect and revert to local/Docker execution. Use action='status' to check the current connection.

Authentication (tried in order):
1. password — if provided
2. key_path — explicit path to a private key file
3. Auto-discovery — tries ~/.ssh/id_* keys and ssh-agent (same as the `ssh` CLI)

action.description: Action to perform. 'open' (default) connects to the server. 'close' disconnects. 'status' shows current connection info.

host.description: Hostname or IP address of the remote server. Required for action='open'.

password.description: SSH password. If omitted, key-based auth is used.

key_path.description: Path to a private key file (e.g. ~/.ssh/id_rsa). If omitted, keys are auto-discovered from ~/.ssh/ and ssh-agent.

passphrase.description: Passphrase for the private key, if encrypted.

system_prompt:
You have the `sshc` tool to connect to remote servers via SSH.

IMPORTANT: Once you call `sshc(action='open')`, ALL subsequent `bash` and `runpy` calls will execute ON THE REMOTE SERVER, not locally. File tools (read_file, write_file, etc.) still operate locally — use bash with cat/tee/scp for remote file operations.

To return to local execution, call `sshc(action='close')`.

Connection details can be configured in agent variables (SSH_HOST, SSH_USERNAME, SSH_PASSWORD, SSH_KEY_PATH, SSH_PASSPHRASE). If these are set, you can simply call `sshc()` without arguments to connect. You can also override any variable by passing explicit arguments.
